channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: I Was The Only Thing Connecting Claude, ChatGPT, and Codex. So I Built My Replacement.
executive_summary: Nate B Jones, formerly the sole human connector for advanced AI models like Claude, ChatGPT, and Codex, built an 'Open Engine' to automate this critical orchestration. This how-to guide detailed the process, emphasizing that the engine took '4-5 months' to develop, can find 'hundreds' of zero-day vulnerabilities, and advocates for a '50% code hygiene' evaluation split. He forecasts that 'implementation costs' for such powerful AI systems are 'going to zero,' making them more accessible.
bullets: Nate B Jones stated he was "The Only Thing Connecting Claude, ChatGPT, and Codex" before he developed his "Open Engine." His direct human intervention was a bottleneck, limiting how fast and efficiently these powerful AI models could work together on complex projects.

The primary purpose of the "Open Engine" is to replace Nate's human role in orchestrating advanced AI models like Claude, ChatGPT, and Codex. This engine autonomously manages the interactions and data flow between specialized AIs, allowing them to collaborate on tasks without constant manual oversight.

Nate highlighted that the security assurance "A good human engineer wrote this" is becoming "a much weaker security claim" with the rise of AI-generated code. This means code, regardless of its origin, must undergo rigorous, automated security evaluations to ensure its integrity and safety.

The core functional development of the "Open Engine" took Nate B Jones approximately "4-5 months" to complete. This timeline demonstrates that building sophisticated AI orchestration systems is a significant but achievable engineering effort for dedicated developers.

Nate committed to incorporating advanced self-healing capabilities into the "Open Engine" by "the end of the year." This planned enhancement will enable the engine to automatically detect and rectify issues within its AI orchestration, drastically improving its reliability and minimizing downtime.

For robust AI system development, Nate advocates a radical shift in evaluation, stating that "at least 50% of your eval tests must check code hygiene," contrary to the common 80% functionality / 20% hygiene split. Prioritizing code quality, readability, and structure ensures long-term maintainability, security, and lower debugging costs.

Nate projected that the "implementation costs" for deploying and operating powerful AI engines like his "Open Engine" are "going to zero." This reduction in financial barriers means advanced AI solutions are becoming increasingly accessible, democratizing their development and application.

During simulated tests, the "Open Engine" was able to identify "hundreds" of potential zero-day vulnerabilities within a proprietary codebase. This capability underscores its potential for highly effective, automated security auditing, uncovering critical flaws that might elude human experts or conventional tools.

A key feature of the "Open Engine" is its dynamic ability to select the most appropriate LLM (e.g., Claude, ChatGPT, or Codex) for a specific task. Instead of a one-size-fits-all approach, it intelligently routes requests to the AI best suited for accuracy, speed, or cost, optimizing overall performance.

Nate emphasized a critical multi-stage "red teaming" methodology for ensuring AI safety within the engine. This process involves intentionally challenging the AI with adversarial inputs and scenarios to proactively identify and mitigate vulnerabilities or undesirable behaviors before real-world deployment.

The "Open Engine" was designed to be fundamentally "agnostic to the underlying model," meaning it can seamlessly integrate new LLMs as they emerge. This future-proof architecture allows teams to continuously leverage the latest advancements in AI technology without requiring extensive system redesigns.

Nate stressed the necessity for teams to re-evaluate their entire "SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle)" to effectively integrate AI capabilities. This isn't just about adding tools, but rethinking how AI transforms planning, development, testing, and deployment processes to maximize efficiency and security.

Nate set a critical reliability target of achieving "99.9% uptime" for the "Open Engine" by "Christmas" of the current year. This high availability goal signifies the engine's intended role as an indispensable and continuously operational component within complex AI-driven infrastructure.
key_takeaways: Set a rule that at least 50% of your eval tests must check code hygiene (naming, structure, function length) — not just whether the code works. Most teams currently have this split backwards at 80% functionality / 20% hygiene, which leads to unmanageable, brittle systems down the line.

Start immediately by experimenting with orchestrating multiple specialized AI models like Claude, ChatGPT, and Codex for your projects, leveraging existing frameworks or beginning to build your own 'Open Engine' concepts. If you delay, you will fall behind competitors who are already realizing immense gains in efficiency and innovation from these integrated AI workflows.

When designing new AI systems, always aim for a 'model-agnostic' architecture so you can easily swap out or integrate new large language models as they evolve. Failing to do so will lock you into outdated technology and necessitate costly, time-consuming re-engineering efforts with every significant AI advancement.

technical_terms.Open Engine: A system built by Nate B Jones that automates the coordination and intelligent selection of various advanced AI models, like Claude, ChatGPT, and Codex, for specific tasks. Think of it like a smart command center that directs specialized AI agents.
technical_terms.Claude: An advanced large language model (LLM) developed by Anthropic, often recognized for its strong performance in complex reasoning, nuanced understanding, and conversational abilities. Think of it like a highly articulate and thoughtful AI consultant.
technical_terms.ChatGPT: A widely known large language model (LLM) from OpenAI, proficient in generating human-like text, answering questions, writing code, and engaging in diverse conversations. Think of it like a versatile AI wordsmith and knowledge base rolled into one.
technical_terms.Codex: An AI model developed by OpenAI, specifically trained on code to generate, understand, and complete programming tasks. Think of it like a brilliant AI junior developer who can instantly write snippets or help debug.
technical_terms.Large Language Model (LLM): An AI model trained on massive datasets of text to comprehend, create, and process human language, enabling tasks like translation, summarization, and interactive conversation. Think of it like a super-intelligent librarian who has read every book and can then summarize or write new ones.
technical_terms.Orchestration: The automated coordination, management, and sequencing of multiple distinct AI models or software components to work together seamlessly toward a larger goal. Think of it like a maestro conducting an entire orchestra, ensuring each instrument plays its part at the right time.
technical_terms.Zero-day vulnerability: A critical security flaw in software that is unknown to the vendor, meaning there are 'zero days' for them to develop and release a patch before malicious attackers might exploit it. Think of it like a secret, hidden weak point in a fortress that even its builders are unaware of.
technical_terms.Code Hygiene: The practice of writing clear, readable, well-structured, and consistently formatted code, making it easier for humans to understand, maintain, debug, and collaborate on. Think of it like keeping a kitchen meticulously clean and organized, which makes cooking safer, more efficient, and more enjoyable.
technical_terms.Implementation Costs: The expenses incurred in setting up, integrating, customizing, and deploying a new software system or AI solution within an existing technical environment. Think of it like the initial investment to get a new factory up and running, including machinery, setup, and staff training.
technical_terms.Red Teaming: A rigorous security practice where a team simulates adversarial attacks against a system to identify vulnerabilities, weaknesses, and potential exploitation pathways from an attacker's perspective. Think of it like hiring a team of expert safe-crackers to test your new bank vault before it holds real money, revealing any design flaws.
technical_terms.Model-agnostic: A system design principle where a system is built to function independently of the specific underlying AI models it uses, allowing for easy swapping, upgrading, or integration of different models. Think of it like a universal remote control that can operate any brand of television, DVD player, or sound system.
technical_terms.SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle): A structured process encompassing the entire lifespan of software, from initial planning and design through development, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Think of it like a detailed blueprint and project plan for constructing a building, guiding every stage from foundation to final touches.
